learn.title: Floating Solar: Harvesting Energy from Water
learn.intro: Floating solar farms, or 'floatovoltaics', solve the land-scarcity dilemma by placing panels on reservoirs and lakes. This technology is vital for the energy transition because it maximizes space efficiency and creates a symbiotic relationship between energy production and water preservation, reducing the need to clear forests or displace agriculture for large-scale solar parks.
learn.c1_text: Panels act like a giant sunshade for the water. By blocking sunlight, they significantly reduce evaporation rates, similar to how a lid keeps water in a pot. This preserves precious freshwater resources in drought-prone areas while generating clean electricity simultaneously.
learn.c2_text: Water acts as a natural coolant. Because solar panels lose efficiency as they overheat, the cooling effect of the underlying water keeps the cells at a lower temperature, increasing energy output by up to 5-10% compared to land-based systems.
learn.c3_text: India is rapidly adopting this via projects like the Omkareshwar Dam project. The government's focus on integrating floating solar with existing hydropower reservoirs allows for shared transmission infrastructure, reducing overall project costs and speeding up deployment across various states.
learn.stat2: Land saving: 100% reduction in land use
learn.stat4: Evaporation reduction: up to 70% in some reservoirs

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries: Circular Energy
learn.intro: Electric vehicle batteries are typically retired when they hit 70-80% capacity—too low for a car, but perfect for the grid. Repurposing these batteries prevents premature waste and lowers the cost of energy storage, creating a circular economy that reduces the demand for newly mined lithium and cobalt.
learn.c1_text: Think of it like a retired athlete who becomes a coach. The battery can no longer sprint (power a car), but it can still walk (store grid energy). These batteries are grouped into large arrays to store wind or solar power for later use.
learn.c2_text: These repurposed packs provide 'peak shaving,' absorbing excess energy during the day and releasing it during evening spikes. This reduces the strain on the power grid and prevents blackouts without needing to build expensive new power plants.
learn.c3_text: With India's push for EV adoption under FAME II, the volume of retired batteries will surge. Indian startups are now developing 'Battery Energy Storage Systems' (BESS) using second-life cells to power rural microgrids and solar-powered irrigation pumps.
learn.stat2: Cost reduction: 30-50% cheaper than new batteries
learn.stat3: India: FAME II driving EV growth
learn.stat4: Life extension: adds 5-10 years of utility

learn.title: Flow Batteries: The Grid's Giant Reservoirs
learn.intro: Unlike standard batteries, flow batteries store energy in liquid electrolytes kept in external tanks. This decoupling of power and energy makes them ideal for grid-scale storage, as increasing capacity is as simple as building a larger tank, providing a scalable solution for long-term energy security.
learn.c1_text: Imagine two giant water tanks connected by a pump. Instead of water, they hold chemical electrolytes. When the liquids flow across a membrane, electricity is generated. To store more energy, you just buy a bigger tank, not a whole new battery.
learn.c2_text: Vanadium flow batteries are popular because they don't degrade over time. Iron-based systems are an even cheaper, non-toxic alternative. Both avoid the fire risks associated with lithium-ion batteries, making them safer for massive urban installations.
learn.c3_text: India's National Energy Storage Policy emphasizes diverse storage technologies. Flow batteries are being explored to balance the massive solar parks in Rajasthan and Gujarat, ensuring a steady power supply even during non-sunny hours.
learn.stat2: Degradation: near-zero capacity loss over decades
learn.stat4: Scalability: Energy capacity decoupled from power output

learn.title: Hydrogen Color Codes: Blue, Green, and Grey
learn.intro: Hydrogen is a versatile energy carrier, but its environmental impact depends entirely on how it is produced. The 'color code' helps us distinguish between carbon-intensive methods and truly sustainable ones, guiding global investments toward the cleanest possible fuel for heavy industry and shipping.
learn.c1_text: Grey hydrogen is made from natural gas and releases CO2—like a factory with a smokestack. Blue hydrogen uses the same process but adds a 'filter' (Carbon Capture and Storage) to trap the CO2 before it hits the atmosphere.
learn.c2_text: Green hydrogen uses renewable electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen via electrolysis. It is completely carbon-free, acting like a 'liquid battery' that can store renewable energy for months or transport it across oceans.
learn.c3_text: India aims to become a global hub for Green Hydrogen. The National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MMT of green hydrogen production by 2030, leveraging India's vast solar capacity to lower production costs.
learn.stat2: Grey Hydrogen: accounts for ~95% of current production
learn.stat3: Electrolysis: splits H2O using renewable power
learn.stat4: Carbon Capture: Blue H2 reduces CO2 by 80-90%

learn.title: Iron-Air Batteries: 100+ Hour Storage
learn.intro: Intermittency is the biggest hurdle for renewables; the sun doesn't always shine and wind doesn't always blow. Iron-air batteries provide 'long-duration energy storage' (LDES), capable of powering the grid for days or weeks, filling the gap that short-term lithium batteries cannot.
learn.c1_text: These batteries work through 'reversible rusting.' During discharge, the battery takes in oxygen from the air and turns iron into rust. During charging, an electric current turns the rust back into iron. It's like a clock that winds and unwinds.
learn.c2_text: While lithium-ion lasts 4-8 hours, iron-air can last 100+ hours. This is critical for 'dark doldrums'—periods of several days with no wind or sun—ensuring the grid remains stable without relying on coal or gas backup.
learn.c3_text: As India targets 500 GW of non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030, LDES is essential. Iron-air batteries are attractive for India due to the abundance of iron ore, reducing reliance on imported rare-earth minerals.
learn.stat2: Cost: significantly cheaper than lithium-ion per kWh
learn.stat4: Material: uses abundant, low-cost iron and air

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespans and Blade Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines provide clean energy for 20-25 years, but their end-of-life management is a growing challenge. Because blades are made of complex composite materials, they are difficult to recycle, making sustainable decommissioning a priority for a truly green energy cycle.
learn.c1_text: A turbine's life is like a car's; it needs regular oil changes and inspections. After 25 years, the mechanical parts are replaced (repowering), but the massive blades often reach their structural limit and must be decommissioned.
learn.c2_text: Blades are made of fiberglass and resins that don't melt or dissolve easily. New techniques include 'pyrolysis' (heating without oxygen) or grinding them into filler for cement and asphalt to keep them out of landfills.
learn.c3_text: India has one of the world's largest wind installations, primarily in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. As early installations age, India is developing policies for 'repowering' old turbines with newer, more efficient models to boost capacity.
learn.stat4: Repowering: can increase energy yield by 2-3x
